topic: The Eye In The Sky - The James Webb Telescope
title: The Eye In The Sky - The James Webb Telescope – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: Imagine a giant EYE looking down at space from high, high above Earth. That EYE is the JAMES WEBB SPACE TELESCOPE, or JWST. Scientists built this amazing TELESCOPE to look at stars, PLANETS, and GALAXIES far, far away. The telescope is named after James E. Webb, a man who helped NASA explore space. It launched into space on December 25, 2021—Christmas Day! Even though it's in space, the telescope sends back beautiful pictures to scientists and children on Earth.

questions.question: What is the telescope that looks at space called?
questions.answer: THE JAMES WEBB SPACE TELESCOPE / JWST

questions.question: When did the James Webb Telescope launch into space?
Name something the text tells us the James Webb Telescope can look at.
Close your eyes and imagine looking through a giant telescope at the stars. What would you feel in your heart when you see them?

hunks.text: The James Webb Telescope is HUGE—about as long as a school bus! Its big MIRROR is made of 18 tiny pieces of shiny gold and beryllium. This special MIRROR helps the telescope collect light from very far away stars. The MIRROR works like a giant net catching light that has traveled for billions of years. When starlight hits the mirror, it bounces and shows us pictures of things we have never seen before. Scientists use these pictures to learn about the universe and how stars were born.

questions.question: How many pieces make up the James Webb Telescope's mirror?
What is the telescope's mirror made from?
How long would you say a school bus is—very long or very short?
Imagine you are holding the James Webb Telescope's shiny golden mirror in your hands. Describe what you see reflected in it.
questions.answer: YOUR FACE / LIGHT / STARS / THE SUN / YOUR CLASSROOM / YOURSELF

hunks.text: The telescope needs very special PROTECTION to work in cold, empty space. Scientists wrapped it in a giant SUNSHIELD made of five thin layers, like a blanket made of silver and gold foil. The SUNSHIELD keeps the telescope cool so the MIRROR and INSTRUMENTS stay just right—colder than ice cream in a freezer! Without this PROTECTION, the Sun's heat would make the telescope too warm, and it would not be able to see the stars clearly. The SUNSHIELD is as big as a tennis court, and it folds up small enough to fit inside the rocket that launches it.

questions.question: How many layers does the sunshield have?
What materials is the sunshield made from?
questions.answer: SILVER AND GOLD FOIL / SILVER / GOLD FOIL

questions.question: Tell me something the sunshield does for the telescope.
questions.answer: KEEPS IT COOL / PROTECTS IT / BLOCKS THE SUN / KEEPS IT THE RIGHT TEMPERATURE

questions.question: Think about how the telescope wraps up in its sunshield like a burrito. What does being wrapped up make you feel—safe or scared?

hunks.text: The JAMES WEBB TELESCOPE lives very far from Earth—one million miles away! That place is called the LAGRANGE POINT, or L2. It is so far that even a super-fast airplane could not reach it in your whole lifetime. The telescope orbits in a special spot in space where the gravity from Earth and the Sun work together perfectly. This special LOCATION keeps the telescope steady and in the perfect position to see the stars. From L2, the telescope can look back in time at light that left stars billions of years ago—light that is still traveling through space.

questions.question: How far away does the James Webb Telescope live from Earth?
What is the special place in space called where the telescope stays?
If something is one million miles away, is it close to you or very far from you?
Would you like to travel one million miles into space to visit the James Webb Telescope, or would you rather stay on Earth?

hunks.text: Scientists control the James Webb Telescope from Earth using RADIO SIGNALS and COMPUTERS. Even though the telescope is one million miles away, scientists can tell it where to look and what to study. The INSTRUMENTS inside the telescope are like special cameras and tools that see different kinds of light—some light we can see with our eyes, and some we cannot. These INSTRUMENTS send PICTURES back to Earth through SIGNALS that travel through space. It takes about 13 minutes for a signal to go from Earth to the telescope and back. The scientists are like space detectives, using the telescope to solve mysteries about space!

questions.question: How do scientists control the telescope from Earth?
questions.answer: RADIO SIGNALS AND COMPUTERS / RADIO SIGNALS / COMPUTERS

questions.question: How long does it take for a signal to go from Earth to the telescope and back?
Name something the instruments inside the telescope can do.
questions.answer: SEE DIFFERENT KINDS OF LIGHT / TAKE PICTURES / SEND PICTURES BACK / STUDY SPACE

questions.question: Imagine you are a space detective sending a radio signal out into space. What sound might that signal make as it zooms away?
questions.answer: BEEP BEEP / WHOOSH / BUZZ / PING / ZAP

hunks.text: The James Webb Telescope shows us pictures of BABY STARS being born inside colorful clouds of gas and dust. These clouds are called NEBULAS, and they look like rainbows painted across space! Scientists can see baby stars that are still wrapped in their dusty blankets, getting ready to grow big and bright. The telescope also looks at EXOPLANETS—planets that orbit other stars, far, far away from our Sun. Some of these faraway planets might have water and be just right for life. The PICTURES from the telescope help us understand that space is full of amazing things we never knew about before.

questions.question: What are colorful clouds of gas and dust in space called?
What word means planets that orbit stars far, far away from our Sun?
Name something the James Webb Telescope can see in space.
questions.answer: BABY STARS / NEBULAS / EXOPLANETS / RAINBOWS OF GAS AND DUST / PLANETS WITH WATER

questions.question: If you could look through the James Webb Telescope and find one amazing thing in space, what would you want to see?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE / STARS / PLANETS / ALIENS / NEBULAS / GALAXIES

hunks.text: Getting the James Webb Telescope to work was like solving the world's biggest puzzle! SCIENTISTS and ENGINEERS from around the world worked together for 25 years to build and test it. The telescope cost about ten billion dollars—imagine if you spent your allowance for 100,000 years! When it finally launched, people all around the world watched and cheered. Even though building it was hard and took so long, the JAMES WEBB TELESCOPE finally gave us PICTURES that are more beautiful and clearer than we ever dreamed. The whole world celebrates every amazing picture it sends back to us from space.

questions.question: How many years did scientists and engineers work to build the James Webb Telescope?
If the James Webb Telescope cost about ten billion dollars, how many dollars is that?
Name something the text tells us about the scientists and engineers who built the telescope.
questions.answer: THEY WORKED TOGETHER / THEY WORKED FROM AROUND THE WORLD / THEY WORKED FOR 25 YEARS / THEY TESTED IT

questions.question: Imagine you are watching the James Webb Telescope launch into space on Christmas morning. What do you hear and feel in that moment?
questions.answer: CHEERING / EXCITEMENT / LOUD NOISES / HAPPINESS / WONDER / THE RUMBLE OF THE ROCKET

hunks.text: Every day, the James Webb Telescope teaches us new things about space and the UNIVERSE. PICTURES from the telescope show us galaxies so far away that their light left them billions of years ago—so we are actually looking back in TIME! Scientists use these PICTURES to understand how stars and planets form. Kids around the world can see these PICTURES online and dream about exploring space someday. The James Webb Telescope is like a TIME MACHINE made of light, helping all of us become SPACE EXPLORERS in our hearts. NASA shares the pictures on websites so anyone can look at them and feel the wonder of the universe.

questions.question: What do pictures from the James Webb Telescope show us about time?
questions.answer: WE ARE LOOKING BACK IN TIME / WE SEE LIGHT FROM BILLIONS OF YEARS AGO / WE CAN SEE THE PAST

questions.question: Name a way that the James Webb Telescope helps us.
questions.answer: TEACHES US ABOUT SPACE / SHOWS US PICTURES / HELPS US UNDERSTAND HOW STARS FORM / HELPS US UNDERSTAND HOW PLANETS FORM / HELPS US BECOME SPACE EXPLORERS

questions.question: Can you look at pictures from the James Webb Telescope online if you are a kid?
How does it make you feel when you think about the James Webb Telescope looking at space and showing us the universe?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE / AMAZED / CURIOUS / EXCITED / INSPIRED / SMALL / WONDER
